title: QAT + Architecture Exploration (Non-Record)
body:
## Current Finding

I implemented QAT with STE in the MLX training stack to reduce degradation from post-training quantization. Early baseline runs suggest the pre-quant vs post-quant val_bpb gap widens over longer training, reaching 0.0325 BPB in a 4-hour baseline, which makes deployment-aware training increasingly important for competitive runs.

### Implementation

- Adds `fake_quantize_per_row()` to simulate int8 per-row quantization during training
- Threads training flag through `CastedLinear` -> `CausalSelfAttention` -> `MLP` -> `Block` -> `GPT` -> `loss`
- Evaluation uses unquantized weights; only the training forward pass sees simulated quantization

## Hypothesis

Training with fake quantization in the loop should reduce the final quantization gap and improve the deployed model's post-quantized validation BPB under the 16MB artifact constraint.

## Why Compute Is Needed

Meaningful validation requires full 10-minute 8xH100 runs and multi-seed significance testing. Local Apple Silicon is sufficient for correctness checks and short smoke tests, but not for the training scale needed to validate small BPB gains.

## Planned Extensions

- SEQUENCE parameter sharing (Takase & Kiyono 2023) for wider models with shared depth
- BitNet b1.58 ternary quantization exploration
- NorMuon optimizer, value embeddings, vocabulary tuning

## Test Plan

- [x] Module imports without errors (`from train_gpt_mlx import GPT`)
- [x] QAT code passes spec compliance review
- [x] QAT code passes code quality review
- [ ] Full 10-min 8xH100 run validates improvement
- [ ] 3-seed significance test (p < 0.01)

comments.body:
**<sub><sub>![P1 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P1-orange?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Split the fake-quant training loss from evaluation**

Because `compiled_loss` is the function that `eval_val()` and the final `final_int8_zlib_roundtrip` check call, hard-wiring `training=True` here makes every validation pass run through `fake_quantize_per_row()` too. That means the logged `val_loss`/`val_bpb` no longer represent the full-precision model during training, and after reloading the dequantized int8 checkpoint the round-trip check fake-quantizes those weights a second time. If the intent is “train with QAT, evaluate without it”, this needs a separate eval path.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Fake-quantize the tied embedding matrix too**

The new QAT path only wraps `CastedLinear.weight`, but the tied `tok_emb.weight` is still used in full precision for both the input embedding lookup and the LM-head projection. `quantize_state_dict_int8()` will nevertheless export that tensor as int8 whenever it exceeds `INT8_KEEP_FLOAT_MAX_NUMEL` (the default 1024×512 table already does), so a large chunk of the deployed quantization error is never present in the training objective. In practice the post-export gap can stay dominated by the embedding table even though the linear layers were trained with QAT.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Fake-quantize the input embedding path too**

This change now fake-quantizes `tok_emb.weight` only for the LM-head reuse here, but `GPT.__call__()` still starts from `self.tok_emb(input_ids)` in full precision. Because `quantize_state_dict_int8()` exports that same tied tensor as int8 for the default 1024×512 table, the deployed model sees quantization error on every input lookup as well as on the logits projection. Training therefore still omits a large, always-on source of post-export distortion, so the measured QAT gain can remain overly optimistic even after this partial fix.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Skip fake-quantizing matrices that stay float at export**

For smaller architecture sweeps, this branch fake-quantizes every `CastedLinear` weight unconditionally, but `quantize_state_dict_int8()` only converts tensors larger than `INT8_KEEP_FLOAT_MAX_NUMEL` and writes smaller matrices to `passthrough`. In configurations like `MODEL_DIM<=256`, several attention/MLP weights fall under that cutoff, so QAT trains against noise that will never exist in the serialized model. That can hurt convergence and make QAT-vs-baseline comparisons misleading for the exact exploration runs this commit introduces.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P1 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P1-orange?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Use exporter clipping in fake_quantize_per_row**

`quantize_float_array()` clips each 2D row at `INT8_CLIP_Q` before computing its int8 scale, but this new training path derives `scale` from the absolute row max and never clips. When a row contains even one large outlier (a realistic case for embeddings and projection weights), QAT trains against a much coarser quantizer than `quantize_state_dict_int8()` will actually serialize, so the measured pre/post-quantization gap no longer reflects the deployed model. If this experiment is intended to validate deployment-aware training, the fake-quant path needs to mirror the exporter’s clipping rule.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.
